content:
First, observe that if \(n\) has any digit 0 then the left‐hand side (the product of its digits) is 0, while the right‐hand side
\[
n^2-15n-27
\]
for positive \(n\) never vanishes at an integer.  Hence none of the digits of \(n\) can be 0.

Next, for a \(k\)–digit number \(n\), the product of its digits is at most \(9^k\), whereas
\[
n^2-15n-27\sim n^2
\]
grows quadratically.  One checks that once \(n\ge37\), 
\[
n^2-15n-27>9^2=81\ge9^k,
\]
so no solutions can occur for \(n\ge37\).  Thus we need only check
\[
17\le n\le36
\]
(because for \(n<17\), \(n^2-15n-27\) is negative and the digit‐product is nonnegative).

A quick direct check of the two‐digit \(n\) from 17 up to 36 shows that the only one satisfying
\[
\text{(product of digits of }n)=n^2-15n-27
\]
is
\[
n=17,\quad 1\cdot7=7,\quad 17^2-15\cdot17-27=289-255-27=7.
\]
No other \(n\) in the range works.  Hence the only positive solution is \(n=17\), and so the sum of all such \(n\) is

\boxed{17}

\boxed{17}
